pathophysiology_summary: Acute rhinosinusitis is inflammation of the paranasal sinus mucosa, usually viral, following a URI; mucosal oedema obstructs the osteomeatal complex causing facial pain/pressure worse on bending, congestion, purulent discharge, hyposmia and maxillary dental ache. Most cases are viral and self-limiting over 10–14 days; purulent discharge alone does not indicate bacterial infection. Bacterial rhinosinusitis is suggested by symptoms >10 days without improvement, 'double-sickening', high fever with purulence, or severe unilateral pain. The ground-truth task is (1) viral vs bacterial discrimination to avoid unnecessary antibiotics, and (2) exclusion of orbital cellulitis (periorbital oedema, proptosis, diplopia, painful/limited eye movements, visual loss) and intracranial complications (severe headache, meningism, confusion).

differential.evidence_basis: Post-URI facial pressure worse on bending, purulent discharge, hyposmia at day 5 is typical viral sinusitis.
Must be monitored; not yet met (<10 days, no double-sickening).
Screen for periorbital swelling, proptosis, diplopia, painful eye movements — sight-threatening complication.
Maxillary tooth ache overlaps; diffuse sinus pattern and absence of focal gingival swelling argue against.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Viral sinusitis leads; orbital/intracranial complications must be actively excluded and antibiotics withheld unless bacterial criteria met.

differential.evidence_basis: No orbital or intracranial features, <10 days, no double-sickening — viral, self-limiting.
No periorbital/eye signs; remains escalation target if they develop.
Reassess at day 10 or if double-sickening; then consider antibiotics.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After a negative complication screen, viral sinusitis leads; symptomatic care with analgesia, saline and short decongestant, no antibiotics.

differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis; reassess for bacterial sinusitis at day 10 or with double-sickening.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final position is self-limiting viral sinusitis.

red_flags.description: Orbital involvement — periorbital swelling/redness, proptosis, diplopia, painful/limited eye movements, visual change
red_flags.clinical_significance: Orbital cellulitis is a sight- and life-threatening complication of sinusitis requiring emergency IV antibiotics/imaging.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Vision loss, cavernous sinus thrombosis, intracranial spread.

red_flags.description: Intracranial involvement — severe unremitting headache, neck stiffness, confusion, focal deficit
Bacterial sinusitis features — >10 days, double-sickening, high fever with purulence

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Equating yellow-green discharge with bacterial infection and prescribing antibiotics before 10 days / double-sickening.
Not screening for periorbital/eye-movement signs in severe sinus pain.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Anterior rhinoscopy of the middle meatus
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Symptomatic self-care; ED/000 for orbital or intracranial features; GP review at day 10 or double-sickening.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Prescribing antibiotics for viral sinusitis; missing early orbital cellulitis.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Most acute rhinosinusitis is viral; manage symptomatically (analgesia, saline irrigation, short-course topical decongestant). Antibiotics only if symptoms >10 days without improvement, double-sickening, or severe features. Refer urgently if orbital/intracranial complications.
